content:
6 min read
Just now

--

You used FFmpeg today. I’d put money on it. You didn’t type it, you didn’t install it on purpose, but it was there, decoding a video in your browser, generating a thumbnail in some app, sitting three dependencies deep under a library you actually did install.
It’s the thing that turns the messy reality of every audio and video format on earth into pixels and sound. It is, quietly, one of the load-bearing walls of the internet.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

It’s maintained by something like a few dozen volunteers. The core of it, the part that does the dangerous work of parsing untrusted bytes from strangers, is closer to a handful.
This week a security startup called Depthfirst pointed an autonomous AI agent at it and found 21 zero-day vulnerabilities. The whole run cost about a thousand dollars.
Let me say the number that actually made me put my coffee down. One of those bugs, a stack buffer overflow in the code that handles service description tables (CVE-2026–39214, if you want to look it up), has been sitting in FFmpeg since 2003. Twenty-three years.
Through two decades of continuous fuzzing, through Google throwing its OSS-Fuzz infrastructure at this exact codebase, through expert humans reading it on purpose looking for precisely this. Nobody found it. An agent reading the code in basically one pass did, and it did it for the price of a nice dinner for four.

What it actually did
The agent went through roughly 1.5 million lines of C. C, the language where a single off-by-one in a parser is the difference between plays the video and runs the attacker’s code.
It didn’t fuzz in the traditional sense, throwing random garbage at the input and waiting for a crash. Depthfirst’s writeup describes something closer to how a really good, really tireless human reviewer works: build a threat model, form hypotheses about where the bugs probably are, then actually execute the code to confirm them. It came back with reproducible proof-of-concept inputs for each one. They put the PoCs on GitHub.
Nine of the 21 already have CVE numbers, CVE-2026–39210 through 39218. The rest are being worked through triage.
The one that I keep thinking about as a working engineer is DFVULN-127, in the AV1 RTP depack etizer. It’s an unauthenticated remote code execution.
The trigger is a single 183-byte network packet. You’re playing an RTSP stream, the kind of thing a security camera or a video-conferencing backend does without anyone watching, and one small malformed packet overflows a heap buffer, corrupts a function pointer in the structure sitting next to it, and now whoever sent the packet is running code on the box. No login, no click. Just 183 bytes.
Most of the others are the same family, heap and stack overflows in the demuxers and decoders.
The TS demuxer, VP9, swscale, the RTP handlers, the AVI and RTSP and RTMP parsers. The boring guts. The stuff nobody volunteers to audit because it’s tedious and it’s been fine for fifteen years.

The part that isn’t really about FFmpeg
Okay so on its own, this is a good news story. Depthfirst disclosed it responsibly. FFmpeg gets to patch 23-year-old holes it didn’t know it had. An AI that can read a whole gnarly C codebase in one pass and find real, exploitable, decades-old bugs is genuinely a gift to the defenders.
I want to be clear I’m not doing a scary AI panic here. This is, mostly, the system working.
It’s the economics underneath it that rearranged something in my head.
A couple months back, the scary version of this story was an AI lab saying it had a model so good at finding vulnerabilities it wasn’t sure it could safely release it.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Big, abstract, gated behind a company’s safety team. That was the headline.
The version that actually showed up in my feed this week wasn’t a locked lab model. It was a startup’s blog post and a thousand-dollar cloud bill, running on the same kind of commercially available models you and I rent by the token. The capability didn’t arrive as a controlled disclosure. It arrived as a receipt.
And a thousand dollars is the part that doesn’t un-think itself. Finding a single decent zero-day used to be weeks of an expensive specialist’s time, which is why the people who did it at scale were nation-states and a few well-funded firms.
Twenty-one of them in a weekend for a grand means the audit just stopped being rare. Depthfirst pointed their agent at FFmpeg and emailed everyone first.
Nothing stops the next person pointing the same kind of agent at the same kind of library and selling what it finds, quietly, to someone who is not going to email anyone at all.
That’s the asymmetry that bugs me. The libraries holding up the internet (FFmpeg, and a hundred others like it) are maintained by people doing it for free in their spare time, and they just walked into a world where anyone with a credit card can strip-mine their life’s work for exploitable bugs faster than they can read the diffs.
Defense found these first this round. That’s not a law of nature. That’s luck and one startup’s ethics.

I went and looked
So I did the thing the article quietly dares you to do. I went and checked what’s actually in my own stack.
I have FFmpeg in three places. A thumbnail service that takes whatever video a user uploads, untrusted bytes, the exact attack surface, and grabs a frame. A transcode worker.
And a third spot where it’s bundled inside a Python package I imported for something unrelated and forgot was even there.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The version pinned in the thumbnail service’s Dockerfile was from 2023. I pinned it once, it worked, I never touched it again, because that’s what you do with the boring infrastructure. You treat it like libc.
You assume that because it’s everywhere, somebody competent is on it.
Everywhere, it turns out, mostly means nobody in particular is responsible for it. That’s the whole free-software bargain, and it’s mostly been a small miracle that it works.
It just got cheaper to attack and no cheaper to defend.
I’m not going to rip FFmpeg out. There’s nothing to replace it with, and that’s sort of the point.
What I did instead: figured out everywhere untrusted media hits a decoder in my stuff, put the worst one in a locked-down sandbox so a 183-byte surprise can’t reach anything that matters, set up something to actually tell me when there’s a new FFmpeg release instead of finding out 23 years later, and — this felt almost stupid to type into the donation form — sent the project some money.
Not much. More than I’d sent them before, which was zero, across eight years of shipping their code in production.
Anyway Go look at your lockfile. Find the version of FFmpeg you’re actually running. Mine was older than one of the bugs, and I write software for a living.
